 {"id":"bd-27gv","title":"Add --no-tui flag for scripting and CI environments","description":"## Problem
Dashboard may not work in headless/CI environments.

## Code Review Findings (2026-01-19)

### Partial Support Exists

Found terminal detection in multiple files:
- output/format.go:132-133 - `IsTerminal()` function
- output/success.go:71 - `isStdoutTerminal()`
- output/errors.go:52 - `isStderrTerminal()`
- cli/util.go:38 - Uses isatty library

### Missing: --no-tui Flag

No `--no-tui` or `--json` flags found for the dashboard command.

## Fix Implementation

### 1. Add Flags to Dashboard Command
```go
// cmd/ntm/dashboard.go
var dashboardCmd = &cobra.Command{
    Use:   "dashboard",
    Short: "Interactive dashboard",
    RunE: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
        noTUI, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("no-tui")
        jsonOut, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("json")

        // Auto-detect non-interactive
        if !output.IsTerminal() || os.Getenv("CI") != "" {
            noTUI = true
        }

        if jsonOut {
            return runDashboardJSON()
        }
        if noTUI {
            return runDashboardPlain()
        }
        return runDashboardTUI()
    },
}

func init() {
    dashboardCmd.Flags().Bool("no-tui", false, "Plain text output (no interactive UI)")
    dashboardCmd.Flags().Bool("json", false, "JSON output (implies --no-tui)")
}
```

### 2. Plain Mode Output
```go
func runDashboardPlain() error {
    status := getDashboardStatus()

    fmt.Printf("Fleet: %d/%d agents active\n", status.Active, status.Total)
    fmt.Printf("  Claude: %d  Codex: %d  Gemini: %d\n",
        status.Claude, status.Codex, status.Gemini)

    if status.Alerts > 0 {
        fmt.Printf("\nAlerts: %d critical, %d warning\n",
            status.Critical, status.Warning)
    }

    fmt.Printf("\nBeads: %d ready, %d in-progress\n",
        status.ReadyBeads, status.InProgressBeads)

    return nil
}
```

### 3. JSON Mode Output
```go
func runDashboardJSON() error {
    status := getDashboardStatus()
    return json.NewEncoder(os.Stdout).Encode(status)
}
```

## Unit Tests Required
```go
func TestDashboardNoTUIFlag(t *testing.T) {
    cmd := rootCmd
    cmd.SetArgs([]string{"dashboard", "--no-tui"})

    var out bytes.Buffer
    cmd.SetOut(&out)
    cmd.Execute()

    output := out.String()
    if strings.Contains(output, "\x1b[") {
        t.Error("--no-tui should not contain ANSI codes")
    }
    if !strings.Contains(output, "Fleet:") {
        t.Error("--no-tui should contain fleet status")
    }
}

func TestDashboardJSONFlag(t *testing.T) {
    cmd := rootCmd
    cmd.SetArgs([]string{"dashboard", "--json"})

    var out bytes.Buffer
    cmd.SetOut(&out)
    cmd.Execute()

    var status map[string]interface{}
    if err := json.Unmarshal(out.Bytes(), &status); err != nil {
        t.Errorf("--json output is not valid JSON: %v", err)
    }
}

func TestDashboardCIAutoDetect(t *testing.T) {
    t.Setenv("CI", "true")

    // Should auto-select plain mode in CI
    cmd := rootCmd
    cmd.SetArgs([]string{"dashboard"})

    var out bytes.Buffer
    cmd.SetOut(&out)
    cmd.Execute()

    output := out.String()
    if strings.Contains(output, "\x1b[") {
        t.Error("CI environment should auto-use plain mode")
    }
}
```

## E2E Test
```bash
# Test --no-tui
ntm dashboard --no-tui | grep -q "Fleet:" && echo "PASS" || echo "FAIL"

# Test --json
ntm dashboard --json | jq .fleet.total && echo "PASS" || echo "FAIL"

# Test CI mode
CI=true ntm dashboard 2>&1 | grep -q "Fleet:" && echo "PASS" || echo "FAIL"

# Test piping
ntm dashboard --no-tui | head -1 | grep -q "Fleet:" && echo "PASS" || echo "FAIL"
```

## Verification
- [ ] --no-tui flag outputs plain text
- [ ] --json flag outputs valid JSON
- [ ] CI=1 auto-detects plain mode
- [ ] Piped output works: ntm dashboard --no-tui | grep","notes":"Implemented --no-tui, --json, and --debug flags for dashboard command. Added shouldUsePlainMode() for auto-detecting CI environments, isTUIDebugEnabled() for NTM_TUI_DEBUG support. Plain text and JSON output modes now fully functional.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-19T07:13:49.112776980Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-20T06:08:33.816282755Z","closed_at":"2026-01-20T06:08:29.329890849Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-27gv","depends_on_id":"bd-2g6y","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-19T07:16:42.699965386Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-27gv","depends_on_id":"bd-36he","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-19T07:17:46.595696217Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

-{"id":"bd-28n0d","title":"Implement shell environment detection","description":"Implement shell environment detection.

## What

Detect shell type and common configuration that affects agent operation.

## Output Schema

```json
{
  "shell": {
    "type": "zsh",              // bash, zsh, fish, etc.
    "tmux_plugin_detected": true,  // May cause issues
    "oh_my_zsh_detected": true,    // Common zsh framework
    "config_path": "~/.zshrc"    // Where to look for aliases
  }
}
```

## Detection Methods

### Shell Type
- Read $SHELL environment variable
- Verify against running shell process

### tmux Plugin Detection
Check common plugin locations:
- ~/.oh-my-zsh/plugins/tmux
- ~/.zshrc contains 'plugins=(... tmux ...)'
- ~/.tmux.conf sourced plugins

### oh-my-zsh Detection
- Check for ~/.oh-my-zsh directory
- Check ZSH environment variable

## Why This Matters

tmux plugins can:
- Alias the tmux command
- Add automatic session attachment
- Modify key bindings

Agents need to know to use /usr/bin/tmux directly when plugins detected.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-19T23:40:42.601068157Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-21T05:19:15.116361457Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-28n0d","depends_on_id":"bd-18gwh","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-19T23:40:42.629141697Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2vcs","title":"Feature: Manual scale to target agent counts","description":"## Overview
Provide a safe, **manual** scaling command that wraps existing `ntm add`/`ntm kill` flows to reach a target agent count without introducing autonomous auto-scaling.

## User Story
As a user managing agent fleets, I want to quickly scale my agent counts up or down with a single command so that I can respond to changing workloads without running multiple add/kill commands.

## Rationale
Auto-scaling adds complex workload prediction and can kill useful context. A deterministic "scale to N" command gives the same user value (one command to resize) while preserving control.

## Scope
- `ntm scale <session> --cc=N --cod=N --gmi=N` (desired target counts)
- Computes delta vs current counts and calls `ntm add` / `ntm kill` appropriately
- Never auto-kills user pane; confirm when removing agents
- Optional: `--dry-run` to show planned changes without executing
- Optional: `--force` to skip confirmation on scale-down

## Delta Calculation
```
Current state:
  Claude (cc):  3 agents
  Codex (cod):  2 agents
  Gemini (gmi): 0 agents

Target: --cc=5 --cod=1 --gmi=2

Delta:
  cc:  +2 (spawn 2 new Claude agents)
  cod: -1 (kill 1 Codex agent)
  gmi: +2 (spawn 2 new Gemini agents)
```

## Scale-Up Flow
```bash
$ ntm scale myproject --cc=5

Current Claude agents: 3
Target Claude agents: 5
Action: Spawn 2 new Claude agents

Spawning cc_4... done
Spawning cc_5... done

Scaling complete. Current state:
  cc: 5, cod: 2, gmi: 0
```

## Scale-Down Flow (with confirmation)
```bash
$ ntm scale myproject --cod=1

Current Codex agents: 3
Target Codex agents: 1
Action: Kill 2 Codex agents

WARNING: Scaling down will terminate agents and lose their context.

Agents to terminate:
  - cod_3 (idle for 5m, no pending work)
  - cod_2 (idle for 3m, no pending work)

Proceed? (y/n/select)
> y

Terminating cod_3... done
Terminating cod_2... done

Scaling complete. Current state:
  cc: 3, cod: 1, gmi: 0
```

## Agent Selection for Scale-Down
When scaling down, agents are selected for termination in this order:
1. **Idle agents** - No pending work, longest idle first
2. **Agents without assignments** - No beads assigned
3. **Agents with completed work** - Already finished their tasks
4. **Never auto-kill user pane** - usr_* panes are always protected
5. **Never kill working agents** - Unless --force is specified

## CLI Examples
```bash
# Scale Claude agents to 5
ntm scale myproject --cc=5

# Scale all types at once
ntm scale myproject --cc=3 --cod=2 --gmi=1

# Dry run - show what would change
ntm scale myproject --cc=10 --dry-run

# Force scale-down without confirmation
ntm scale myproject --cc=1 --force

# Scale to zero (kill all agents of type)
ntm scale myproject --cod=0

# Mixed scaling (up and down)
ntm scale myproject --cc=5 --cod=1 --gmi=3

# Robot mode for scripting
ntm scale myproject --cc=5 --format json
```

## Robot Mode Output
```json
{
  "session": "myproject",
  "before": {"cc": 3, "cod": 2, "gmi": 0},
  "after": {"cc": 5, "cod": 1, "gmi": 2},
  "actions": [
    {"type": "spawn", "agent_type": "cc", "count": 2, "agents": ["cc_4", "cc_5"]},
    {"type": "kill", "agent_type": "cod", "count": 1, "agents": ["cod_2"]},
    {"type": "spawn", "agent_type": "gmi", "count": 2, "agents": ["gmi_1", "gmi_2"]}
  ],
  "success": true
}
```

## Error Handling
- If spawn fails: continue with remaining, report failures at end
- If kill fails: continue with remaining, report failures at end
- If target already met: no-op, report "Already at target"
- Partial success: report what succeeded and what failed

```
Scaling partially completed:
  cc: 3 → 5 (success)
  cod: 2 → 1 (success)
  gmi: 0 → 2 (FAILED: API key not configured for Gemini)

To retry failed scaling: ntm scale myproject --gmi=2
```

## Non‑Goals
- No automatic scaling based on load/metrics
- No background daemon
- No predictive scaling
- No cost-based scaling decisions

## Implementation Notes
- Reuse existing `ntm add` and `ntm kill` logic internally
- Run scale-up before scale-down to avoid losing agents then needing them
- Track which agents were scaled for logging
- Validate target counts are non-negative

## Tests
- Unit: delta calculation with various scenarios
- Unit: agent selection algorithm for scale-down
- Unit: edge cases (zero, negative, same as current)
- E2E: scale up from 0 to N
- E2E: scale down from N to 1
- E2E: mixed scale (up and down together)
- E2E: dry-run shows correct plan
- E2E: force flag skips confirmation

## Logging
```go
log.Printf("[E2E-SCALE] session=%s action=start current=%v target=%v", session, current, target)
log.Printf("[E2E-SCALE] delta cc=%+d cod=%+d gmi=%+d", ccDelta, codDelta, gmiDelta)
log.Printf("[E2E-SCALE] spawn agent_type=%s agent_id=%s success=%t", agentType, agentID, success)
log.Printf("[E2E-SCALE] kill agent_type=%s agent_id=%s success=%t reason=%s", agentType, agentID, success, reason)
log.Printf("[E2E-SCALE] session=%s action=complete success=%t spawned=%d killed=%d errors=%d", session, success, spawned, killed, errors)
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Reaches exact target counts deterministically
- [ ] Uses existing add/kill logic (no duplicate code)
- [ ] Safe prompts when scaling down
- [ ] Never auto-kills user pane
- [ ] Dry-run shows plan without executing
- [ ] Force flag skips confirmation
- [ ] Mixed scaling (up and down) works in single command
- [ ] Partial failures are reported clearly
- [ ] Robot mode outputs valid JSON
- [ ] Tests + logging coverage added","notes":"Rescoped from auto‑scaling to manual scale‑to‑target. Keeps feature value while avoiding background workload prediction.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-19T08:41:29.918597716Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-19T20:43:01.030351065Z","close_reason":"Over-engineering: dynamic scaling requires workload monitoring, auto-spawn, and auto-kill logic. Manual scaling with 'ntm spawn' and 'ntm kill' is more controllable.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2vcs","depends_on_id":"bd-10ufs","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-19T20:43:01.030324404Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

- --to-pane targets specific agent

- --strategy=quality picks best match
- Falls back when no idle agents

### Error Cases

- Retry non-existent bead -> error
- No idle agents -> skip or error

## Logging
- Log retry decisions
- Log old vs new assignment
- Log failures

 {"id":"bd-2wih","title":"Task: Add --stagger flag to spawn","description":"## Purpose
Add staggering option to spawn command for rate limit avoidance.

## Flag
ntm spawn myproject --cc=5 --stagger=smart|fixed|none

## Stagger Modes
- smart: Use learned optimal delays from RateLimitTracker
- fixed: Use fixed delay (--stagger-delay=15s)
- none: No staggering (default for backward compatibility)

## Implementation
func (s *SpawnCmd) spawnWithStagger(agents []AgentSpec) error {
    delay := s.rateLimitTracker.GetOptimalDelay(agents[0].Provider)
    
    for i, agent := range agents {
        if i > 0 && s.staggerMode \!= 'none' {
            time.Sleep(delay)
        }
        
        if err := s.spawnAgent(agent); err \!= nil {
            if isRateLimitError(err) {
                s.rateLimitTracker.RecordRateLimit(agent.Provider)
                delay = s.rateLimitTracker.GetOptimalDelay(agent.Provider)
                i-- // Retry
                continue
            }
            return err
        }
        s.rateLimitTracker.RecordSuccess(agent.Provider)
    }
    return nil
}

## Acceptance Criteria
- Staggered spawning works
- Retries on rate limit
- Progress shown during spawn
- Default is backward compatible

## Technical Notes
Add flag to existing spawn command","notes":"## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests: core logic, error paths, edge cases.
- E2E scripts: full CLI/robot flow with detailed log/slog prefixes (e.g., [E2E-<AREA>]) and include session/agent/bead IDs.
- If structured outputs are affected, assert schema stability + deterministic ordering.

-{"id":"bd-2wkrv","title":"Implement timing constants exposure","description":"Implement timing constants exposure.

## Fields

- ctrl_c_gap_ms: 100 (gap between Ctrl-Cs for exit)
- post_exit_wait_ms: 3000 (wait for shell after exit)
- cc_init_wait_ms: 6000 (wait for claude code init)
- prompt_submit_delay_ms: 1000 (delay before prompt)

## Critical Insight

These timings were discovered through operational experience:
- 100ms Ctrl-C gap is REQUIRED (slower doesn't work)
- 6s is typical claude code startup time
- Adjustments may be needed per system

## Configuration Override

Allow config file override for system-specific tuning:
```yaml
timing:
  ctrl_c_gap_ms: 100
  post_exit_wait_ms: 3000
  cc_init_wait_ms: 6000
```

## Source Priority

1. Config file override (if present)
2. Environment variable override
3. - 6s is typical claude code startup time
- Adjustments may be needed per system

## Configuration Override

Allow config file override for system-specific tuning:
```yaml
timing:
  ctrl_c_gap_ms: 100
  post_exit_wait_ms: 3000
  cc_init_wait_ms: 6000
```

## Source Priority

1. Config file override (if present)
2. Environment variable override
3. Built-in defaults","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-19T23:29:35.914887875Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-21T05:20:34.645691478Z","closed_at":"2026-01-21T05:20:34.645598132Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2wkrv","depends_on_id":"bd-18gwh","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-19T23:29:35.943514203Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2wzs","title":"Add ntm send --priority-order - send to agents in order of bead priority (P0 first)","description":"## Overview
Add --priority-order flag to ntm send to deliver prompts to agents in order of bead priority (P0 first, P4 last).

## Problem
When assigning multiple beads, critical work should start first. Default round-robin ignores priority.

## Solution
`ntm send myproject --priority-order` sorts beads by priority before assignment.

## Implementation
```go
func sortByPriority(beads []Bead) []Bead {
    sorted := make([]Bead, len(beads))
    copy(sorted, beads)
    sort.Slice(sorted, func(i, j int) bool {
        return sorted[i].Priority < sorted[j].Priority // 0 = highest
    })
    return sorted
}
```

## Priority Order
- P0 (critical) → sent first
- P1 (high) → sent second
- P2 (medium) → sent third
- P3 (low) → sent fourth
- P4 (backlog) → sent last

## CLI Examples
```bash
# Send in priority order
ntm send myproject --priority-order

# Combine with assignment
ntm assign myproject --priority-order --auto

# Show what would be sent
ntm send myproject --priority-order --dry-run
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] P0 beads sent before P1, etc.
- [ ] Stable sort (same priority maintains order)
- [ ] Works with --batch and assign
- [ ] Logging shows priority order
- [ ] Compatible with --limit

## Tests Required
- Unit test: Priority sorting algorithm
- Unit test: Stable sort within same priority
- E2E test: Priority-ordered batch send","notes":"## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests: core logic, error paths, edge cases.
- E2E scripts: full CLI/robot flow with detailed log/slog prefixes (e.g., [E2E-<AREA>]) and include session/agent/bead IDs.
- If structured outputs are affected, assert schema stability + deterministic ordering.

 {"id":"bd-bh74z","title":"Implement hard kill fallback using kill -9","description":"Implement hard kill fallback when soft exit fails.

## When to Use
When soft exit (Ctrl-C) doesn't work after 3 seconds, need to kill -9 the child process.

## Implementation
1. Get shell PID from tmux list-panes
2. Get child PID via pgrep -P <shell_pid>
3. kill -9 <child_pid>
4. Wait for shell to return to prompt

## Code Pattern
pane_pid=$(tmux list-panes -t session:1 -F '#{pane_index} #{pane_pid}' | grep '^N ' | awk '{print $2}')
child_pid=$(pgrep -P $pane_pid | head -1)
kill -9 $child_pid

## Error Handling
- If no child process: already exited, proceed
- If permission denied: return error with diagnostic info
- If shell doesn't return after kill: serious issue, return detailed error","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-19T23:25:01.791002696Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-19T23:25:01.821005688Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-bh74z","depends_on_id":"bd-21tqw","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-19T23:25:01.820978166Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-dz3r","title":"EPIC: Automatic File Reservation via Agent Mail","description":"**IDEA #5 - INVISIBLE SAFETY NET (87% confidence)**

## What It Does
When NTM detects an agent is editing a file (from pane output analysis), it automatically reserves that file in Agent Mail, preventing other agents from conflicting edits.

## Why This Matters
- Conflicts between agents are a major pain point
- Currently: Users must remember to manually reserve files
- After: It just works; conflicts are prevented automatically
- Users don't even notice until they get a conflict warning (which is now rare)

## How It Works
Detection triggers:
1. Tool use detection: Parse pane output for tool calls that write files
   - Claude: Edit, Write, NotebookEdit tool names
   - Codex: write_file, edit_file patterns
   - Gemini: Similar patterns
2. Path extraction: Extract file paths from tool calls
3. Automatic reservation: Call Agent Mail file_reservation_paths
4. TTL management: Extend reservation while agent continues editing; release on idle

## Flywheel Alignment
- Makes Agent Mail's file reservation feature actually get used
- Most users don't manually reserve files; this makes it automatic
- Increases Agent Mail's value proposition significantly

## Technical Implementation
- New file: internal/watcher/file_reservation.go
- FileReservationWatcher struct with background monitoring
- Detection patterns extended from robot/patterns.go
- Config under [file_reservation] section
- Conflict notification to dashboard/alerts

## Dependencies
Depends on: ntm init (for Agent Mail registration)","notes":"## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests: core logic, error paths, edge cases.
- E2E scripts: full CLI/robot flow with detailed log/slog prefixes (e.g., [E2E-<AREA>]) and include session/agent/bead IDs.
- If structured outputs are affected, assert schema stability + deterministic ordering.

The FileReservationWatcher is now integrated into the dashboard lifecycle and automatically starts when Agent Mail is available and file_reservation.enabled=true in config.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-dz3r","depends_on_id":"bd-38gl","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-19T06:06:17.215604678Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
